question.text: An AMC decides to change the benchmark index of one of its equity schemes from Nifty 50 TRI to Nifty 100 TRI. This change:

answer.text: May be considered a change in fundamental attributes, requiring written notice and exit option at NAV without exit load
Is a routine operational change that requires no communication to unitholders
Requires SEBI to personally approve the new benchmark before implementation
Automatically converts the scheme from a large-cap to a multi-cap category

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** A change in benchmark index can be considered a change in fundamental attributes because the benchmark is part of the scheme's identity disclosed in the SID. If this constitutes a fundamental attribute change, the AMC must provide written notice to unitholders and offer an exit at NAV without exit load.
**The Trap:** Benchmark changes are NOT routine — they can signal a shift in scheme positioning. SEBI does not personally approve individual benchmark changes. Changing the benchmark does not automatically change the scheme category — those are governed by SEBI categorization norms.
**Cross-Exam Context:** NISM V-B and XII test whether candidates understand the breadth of what constitutes "fundamental attributes." Benchmark changes, while seemingly technical, can significantly alter how scheme performance is perceived.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** If a scheme changes its benchmark, investigate why. Sometimes AMCs switch to a broader benchmark to make underperformance less visible. Always evaluate whether the new benchmark is appropriate for the scheme's actual investment universe.
